andidate hits the ground running from day one, never stops raising money, a=

=2E =E2=80=9CUndisciplined candidates can=20=
quickly find a safe district slippi=

After dozens of conversations with members and operatives over several mont=
hs=E2=80=94including party loyalists who candidly, if grudgingly, admitted =
the strengths of the opposing party=E2=80=94here are the candidates who

cle is assuredly Ohio=E2=80=99s Sherrod Brown, despite having to swim upstr=
eam against Trump=E2=80=99s eight-point advantage in the state. Brown, who =
probably has the best home state brand of any senator after three terms, ha=

=80=94the candidates have spent an astronomic $500 million, shattering prev=
ious records for a Senate race=E2=80=94it=E2=80=99s an extraordinary achiev=
ement that he=E2=80=99s still in the race in a state that is racing to the =
right.

water C.E.O. and George W. Bush official who=E2=80=99s trying to dislodge t=
hird-term incumbent Bob Casey in Pennsylvania. McCormick=E2=80=99s campaign=
 has not been without flaws=E2=80=94the carpetbagger tag and missteps like =
mispronouncing Yuengling beer have worked against him=E2=80=94but this is h=
is second statewide campaign in two years (he lost the 2022 Republican Sena=
te primary to Dr. Oz), and Republicans are impressed by the former West Poi=
nt wrestling captain=E2=80=99s ability to regroup.
* The scrappers: These are the battle-tested House candidates whose distric=
ts will always be difficult, but seem to defy political gravity anyway. The=
 first name Republicans mention is New York freshman Mike Lawler, who depos=
ed Sean Patrick Maloney, the D.C.C.C. chairman, last cycle. Perhaps that=E2=
=80=99s why Lawler irritates Democrats in ways no other

operative told me when I said Lawler made the list. (Runner-up: California =
five-termer David Valadao, who came back after losing in 2018 and thus achi=
eved =E2=80=9Cwhite whale=E2=80=9D status as a candidate no one can seem to=
 outsmart or outrun.)

On the Democratic side, the scrappiest prizefighter is Marcy Kaptur of Ohio=
, who, at 78, is the longest-serving woman in Congress, with 21 terms under=
 her belt. Three years ago, Republicans gerrymandered her safe Democratic d=

s running a strong campaign with quality ads that draw attention to her vit=
ality. She=E2=80=99s earned the respect of both Democrats and Republicans h=
alf her age. She won a tough race last cycle and she=E2=80=99s in real dang=
er again=E2=80=94this is her first time in a more hostile district with on =
the ballot with Trump=E2=80=94against Republican Derek Merrin. However, the=
 Cook
Political Report has the seat leaning Democratic. (Runner-up: Pat Ryan of N=
ew York=E2=80=94whom a G.O.P. strategist described to me as =E2=80=9Cfrustr=
atingly good.=E2=80=9D)
* The upstarts: Of course, it=E2=80=99s harder to run against incumbents=E2=
=80=94name identification, stronger fundraising, etcetera=E2=80=94but that =
just encourages some challengers. Tom Barrett of Michigan was the agreed-up=
on Republican winner in this category. He=E2=80=99s running against Democra=
t Curtis Hertel for the vacating seat of Elissa Slotkin=E2=80=94one of Demo=
crats=E2=80=99 best candidates anywhere=E2=80=94whom Barrett gave a run for=
 her money last cycle. Austin Theriault of Maine was the second-most-freque=
ntly mentioned Republican candidate in this category.

For the Democrats, Janelle Stelson is the pick. Operatives also frequently =
cited Will Rollins, but Stelson stands out because she=E2=80=99s running in=

e weak campaign of six-term incumbent Republican Scott Perry, this is now o=

* The safeties: Sometimes, the most valuable incumbents are the ones you ne=
ver hear about. They=E2=80=99re strong fundraisers, workhorses in the distr=
icts, and have reputations that the other side can=E2=80=99t seem to tarnis=
h. They=E2=80=99re able to so completely dominate their races that they fre=
e up their campaign committees and aligned super PACs to spend TV ad money =
elsewhere. For Republicans, that=E2=80=99s Brian Fitzpatrick, who=E2=80=99s=
 in his fourth term and saving Team Red millions by allocating his money to=

ate, and nearby House campaigns.

For Democrats, it=E2=80=99s Tom Suozzi. It=E2=80=99s hard to believe that o=

list George

x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">Every cycle, there =
are a handful of candidates who aren=E2=80=99t merely <i>good</i>, but <i>b=
ona</i> <i>fide</i> M.V.P.s=E2=80=94their success ripples across the electo=
ral map. They put seats in play that shouldn=E2=80=99t be competitive; they=
 fund their own ad campaigns, freeing up money for the party to spend elsew=
here; and they don=E2=80=99t make fatal mistakes. =E2=80=9CYour ideal House=
 candidate hits the ground running from day one, never stops raising money,=
 and stays on message,=E2=80=9D said <b>Michael Kaplan</b>, a veteran of th=
e N.R.C.C. =E2=80=9CUndisciplined candidates can quickly find a safe distri=
ct slipping out of their grasp at the worst time in the cycle.=E2=80=9D<br/=
><br/>After dozens of conversations with members and operatives over severa=
l months=E2=80=94including party loyalists who candidly, if grudgingly, adm=
itted the strengths of the opposing party=E2=80=94here are the candidates w=
ho
make life a little easier for campaign committee chairs <b>Richard Hudson,<=
/b> <b>Suzan DelBene</b>, <b>Steve Daines</b>, and <b>Gary Peters</b>.<br/>=
<br/><ul style=3D"margin:0;color:#000000;font-size:16px;line-height:30px;ma=
rgin-top:0;margin-bottom:0;list-style-type:square;mso-special-format:bullet=
"><li><i>Super senators</i>: The most impressive Democratic Senate candidat=
e of the cycle is assuredly Ohio=E2=80=99s <b>Sherrod Brown</b>, despite ha=
ving to swim upstream against <b>Trump</b>=E2=80=99s eight-point advantage =
in the state. Brown, who probably has the best home state brand of any sena=
tor after three terms, has raised piles of money to secure the cheap candid=
ate rates on TV. Though he could still lose his dead-heat contest with Repu=
blican <b>Bernie</b> <b>Moreno</b>=E2=80=94the candidates have spent an ast=
ronomic<i> $500 million</i>, shattering previous records for a Senate race=
=E2=80=94it=E2=80=99s an extraordinary achievement that he=E2=80=99s still =
in the race in a state that is racing to the right.
<br/><br/>On the G.O.P. side, the award must go to <b>David McCormick</b>, =
the former Bridgewater C.E.O. and <b>George W. Bush</b> official who=E2=80=
=99s trying to dislodge third-term incumbent <b>Bob</b> <b>Casey</b> in Pen=
nsylvania. McCormick=E2=80=99s campaign has not been without flaws=E2=80=94=
the carpetbagger tag and missteps like mispronouncing Yuengling beer have w=
orked against him=E2=80=94but this is his second statewide campaign in two =
years (he lost the 2022 Republican Senate primary to <b>Dr. Oz</b>), and Re=
publicans are impressed by the former West Point wrestling captain=E2=80=99=
s ability to regroup.</li> <br/><li><i>The scrappers</i>: These are the bat=
tle-tested House candidates whose districts will always be difficult, but s=
eem to defy political gravity anyway. The first name Republicans mention is=
 New York freshman <b>Mike Lawler</b>, who deposed <b>Sean Patrick Maloney<=
/b>, the D.C.C.C. <i>chairman, </i>last cycle. Perhaps that=E2=80=99s why L=
awler irritates Democrats in ways no other
Republican can. =E2=80=9CI=E2=80=99m going to vomit,=E2=80=9D a Democratic =
operative told me when I said Lawler made the list. (Runner-up: California =
five-termer <b>David Valadao</b>, who came back after losing in 2018 and th=
us achieved =E2=80=9Cwhite whale=E2=80=9D status as a candidate no one can =
seem to outsmart or outrun.)<br/><br/>On the Democratic side, the scrappies=
t prizefighter is <b>Marcy Kaptur</b> of Ohio, who, at 78, is the longest-s=
erving woman in Congress, with <i>21 terms</i> under her belt. Three years =
ago, Republicans gerrymandered her safe Democratic district into one Trump =
carried in 2020. Kaptur rolled with the redraw and is running a strong camp=
aign with quality ads that draw attention to her vitality. She=E2=80=99s ea=
rned the respect of both Democrats and Republicans half her age. She won a =
tough race last cycle and she=E2=80=99s in real danger again=E2=80=94this i=
s her first time in a more hostile district with on the ballot with Trump=
=E2=80=94against Republican <b>Derek Merrin</b>. However, the Cook
Political Report has the seat leaning Democratic. (Runner-up: <b>Pat Ryan <=
/b>of New York=E2=80=94whom a G.O.P. strategist described to me as =E2=80=
=9Cfrustratingly good.=E2=80=9D) </li><br/><li><i>The upstarts</i>: Of cour=
se, it=E2=80=99s harder to run against incumbents=E2=80=94name identificati=
on, stronger fundraising, etcetera=E2=80=94but that just <i>encourages</i> =
some challengers. <b>Tom Barrett </b>of Michigan was the agreed-upon Republ=
ican winner in this category. He=E2=80=99s running against Democrat <b>Curt=
is</b> <b>Hertel</b> for the vacating seat of <b>Elissa Slotkin</b>=E2=80=
=94one of Democrats=E2=80=99 best candidates <i>anywhere=E2=80=94</i>whom B=
arrett gave a run for her money last cycle. <b>Austin Theriault </b>of Main=
e was the second-most-frequently mentioned Republican candidate in this cat=
egory.<br/><br/>For the Democrats, <b>Janelle Stelson</b> is the pick. Oper=
atives also frequently cited <b>Will Rollins</b>, but Stelson stands out be=
cause she=E2=80=99s running in a Pennsylvania district that should not be i=
n play.
Thanks to her brand=E2=80=94she=E2=80=99s a former TV anchor=E2=80=94and th=
e weak campaign of six-term incumbent Republican <b>Scott Perry</b>, this i=
s now one of the most-watched races in the country.</li> <br/><li><i>The sa=
feties</i>: Sometimes, the most valuable incumbents are the ones you never =
hear about. They=E2=80=99re strong fundraisers, workhorses in the districts=
, and have reputations that the other side can=E2=80=99t seem to tarnish. T=
hey=E2=80=99re able to so completely dominate their races that they free up=
 their campaign committees and aligned super PACs to spend TV ad money else=
where. For Republicans, that=E2=80=99s <b>Brian Fitzpatrick</b>, who=E2=80=
=99s in his fourth term and saving Team Red millions by allocating his mone=
y to the Philly market, where ad rates have been jacked up by presidential,=
 Senate, and nearby House campaigns. <br/><br/>For Democrats, it=E2=80=99s =
<b>Tom Suozzi</b>. It=E2=80=99s hard to believe that only a year ago, his L=
ong Island district was represented by Republican fabulist <b>George
Santos</b>, but Suozzi=E2=80=99s eight-point win in February=E2=80=99s spec=
ial election was solid enough that both parties are pretty much leaving thi=
s one alone, which is saving Democrats a pile of money in the New York City=
 media market. <br/></li></ul></td>

x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">We=E2=80=99re less =
than a week from Election Day, and the mood inside the <b>Trump </b>campaig=
n has undergone yet <i>another </i>transformation. Last week, I <u><a href=
=3D"https://email.puck.news/e/c/eyJlbWFpbF9pZCI6ImRnVDJ4Z1lEQUt2UktxclJLZ0d=
TNVNGRjVheU51cUF4eWlIdGVyZz0iLCJocmVmIjoiaHR0cHM6Ly9wdWNrLm5ld3MvdHJ1bXAtaX=

7c562cf2ba6d29e15ec60b69bf9b9ab423" style=3D"color:#d40000;text-decoration:=
none;font-weight:bold" class=3D"sdf">reported</a></u> on the preemptive but=
 undeniably palpable sense of <i>euphoria</i> washing over Mar-a-Lago as da=
ta rolled in depicting early-voting surges in Nevada, Georgia, and North Ca=
rolina. But now, as the early results from <i>Pennsylvania</i> reveal an in=
flux of first-time female voters who will likely break for <b>Harris</b>, a=
 newfound anxiety is taking hold. While Trump continues to claim he has a m=
assive lead,
setting the stage to contest any unfavorable result, some in the Mar-a-Lago=
-sphere are starting to believe that his surge last week was two weeks prem=
ature.<br/><br/>Pennsylvania is obviously a must-win state for both campaig=
ns=E2=80=A6 but it=E2=80=99s <i>really </i>crucial for Trump. While his inn=
er circle feels confident about winning the Sunbelt, they recognize that th=
ey have a good chance of losing Michigan, where the gender gap is stark and=
 students are coming out in record numbers. (A new CNN poll shows Harris up=
 5 points in the state.) So the situation in Pennsylvania=E2=80=94where wom=
en have outpaced men by 13 points in the early vote=E2=80=94has sent the ca=
mpaign into a tailspin during the past two days. <br/><br/>Not unlike 2020,=
 Trump and his allies are preemptively making outlandish and extreme assert=
ions to lay the groundwork for a claim, if they don=E2=80=99t prevail, that=
 the election was stolen. They=E2=80=99re also engaging in the early stages=
 of election lawfare. =E2=80=9CThey=E2=80=99re going so crazy
here,=E2=80=9D said a campaign source. =E2=80=9CAnyone who hears how rabid =
they are about this issue can=E2=80=99t walk away from this and think they =
feel comfortable about where they=E2=80=99re at in PA. They=E2=80=99re talk=
ing about <i>criminal referrals</i>. They want to find poll watchers who th=
ey feel are engaged in voter suppression so that they can refer criminal pr=
osecutions.=E2=80=9D (An R.N.C. spokesperson didn=E2=80=99t respond to requ=
ests for comment.) <br/><br/>You could almost <i>hear</i> the anxiety in <b=
>Charlie Kirk</b>=E2=80=99s tweets last night, as he implored young bros to=
 turn out. =E2=80=9CEarly vote has been disproportionately female. If men s=
tay at home, Kamala is president. It=E2=80=99s that simple,=E2=80=9D Kirk p=
osted on Twitter/X. =E2=80=9CIf you want a vision of the future if you don=
=E2=80=99t vote, imagine Kamala's voice cackling, forever. Men need to =
GO VOTE NOW.=E2=80=9D <br/><br/>As I <u><a href=3D"https://email.puck.news/=
e/c/eyJlbWFpbF9pZCI6ImRnVDJ4Z1lEQUt2UktxclJLZ0dTNVNGRjVheU51cUF4eWlIdGVyZz0=
iLCJocmVmIjoiaHR0cHM6Ly9wdWNrLm5ld3MvdHJ1bXAtY2FtcGFpZ24tbXVybXVycy1ybmMtYm=
FzaGluZy1tb3JlLWNvcmV5LWxld2FuZG93c2tpLWRyYW1hLz91dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249VGhlK0Jlc=
3QrJTI2K1RoZStCcmlnaHRlc3QrLStTVUJTQ1JJQkVSUyslMjgxMCUyRjMxJTJGMjQlMjlcdTAw=
MjZ1dG1fc291cmNlPWN1c3RvbWVyLmlvIiwiaW50ZXJuYWwiOiJmNmM2MDYxN2RhNDJhYmQxMmE=
iLCJsaW5rX2lkIjoxMTQwMzgzfQ/2250e0d0d150f21a775df237541862300cb45d767303590=
79051df5db511304a" style=3D"color:#d40000;text-decoration:none;font-weight:=
bold" class=3D"sdf">reported</a></u> two weeks ago, Trump has already zeroe=
d in on Republican National Committee chairman <b>Michael Whatley</b> as hi=
s scapegoat if things go south. The lawsuits have also already begun=E2=80=
=94one of which they won in Bucks County, when they complained that a poll =
watcher had turned people away till Election Day because the lines were too=
 long. (The judge ruled in their favor, extending the drop-off day for mail=
-in ballots to November 1.) Democrats, meanwhile, have sued over alleged ba=
llot problems in Erie County, according to Politico. <br/> <br/>And while T=
rump may want to blame Whatley and his =E2=80=9Celection integrity unit=E2=
=80=9D for a loss, the campaign is also preparing to blame outside groups w=
ho were supposed to handle Trump=E2=80=99s ground game. Sure, figureheads l=
ike
Kirk at Turning Point and <b>Elon Musk</b> at America PAC are firmly plante=
d in Trump=E2=80=99s inner circle and would probably walk away unscathed. B=
ut the same can=E2=80=99t be said for <b>Phil Cox</b> and <b>Generra Peck</=
b>, who have essentially commandeered Musk=E2=80=99s America PAC and are se=
en as too closely aligned with Trumpworld=E2=80=99s collective enemy <b>Ron=
 DeSantis</b>. =E2=80=9CWe=E2=80=99re solely focused on winning, and we=E2=
=80=99re not looking at anything else. That=E2=80=99s the only thing that m=
atters,=E2=80=9D said Trump spokesperson <b>Steven Cheung</b>.<br/></td>

antor Fitzgerald C.E.O. <b>Howard Lutnick</b> revealed that <b>Robert F. Ke=
nnedy Jr.</b> had successfully convinced him, over a two-and-a-half-hour co=

 and Human Services agency=E2=80=94seen by many as an apparent <i>quid pro =
quo</i> for his Trump endorsement. <br/><br/>But I=E2=80=99m told it=E2=80=
=99s conventional wisdom inside Mar-a-Lago that it would be impossible to g=
et Kennedy confirmed in the Senate, even <i>if</i> Republicans pick up more=
 than two
seats. More likely, I=E2=80=99m told, he could be expected to wind up with =
a =E2=80=9Cczar=E2=80=9D-like advisory role similar to what=E2=80=99s been =
promised to <b>Elon Musk</b>, who could have free rein running a so-called =
=E2=80=9CDepartment of Government Efficiency=E2=80=9D=E2=80=94an agency tha=
t Trump conjured up the other day. Lutnick also acknowledged in the same in=
terview that R.F.K. wouldn=E2=80=99t make it into the cabinet as H.H.S. sec=
retary. <br/><br/>Clearly, it=E2=80=99s silly season for Trump staffing spe=
culation, especially with the candidate bouncing names off people for feedb=
ack and teasing those around him with posts that even he knows wouldn=E2=80=
=99t be right for them. Most recently, Trump=E2=80=99s longtime friend <b>S=
teve</b> <b>Witkoff</b>, the real estate magnate who famously brokered the =

has managed to get himself on the hypothetical shortlist for White House ch=
ief of staff. Witkoff, who was with Trump during the thwarted assassination=
 plot on the
golf course, spoke at the Madison Square Garden rally. <br/><br/>As much as=
 Trump enjoys his company, many close to the former president say he doesn=
=E2=80=99t have the political bona fides to navigate Washington and actuall=
y pass legislation. <b>Kevin McCarthy</b>, another name in consideration, c=
ould handle that, but he has detractors not only in the Congressional Freed=
om Caucus, but also around Trump=E2=80=94like his nemesis <b>Matt</b> <b>Ga=
etz</b>, who=E2=80=99s been traveling around with the former president. <b>=

ation, but it=E2=80=99s not her style to lobby. <b>Brooke</b> <b>Rollins</b=
>, his former director of the Domestic Policy Council and C.E.O. of America=
 First Institute, is also being considered. <br/><br/>In any event, there=
=E2=80=99s only so much time left for the Trump-adjacent to make their pres=
ence felt on the campaign trail, or to make peace with the candidate. <b>St=

iceably absent from Trump rallies, telling Fox News that she hasn=E2=80=99t=
 spoken to him since July=E2=80=94a conscious decoupling, perhaps, although=

ump asked. Alas, <b>Tucker Carlson</b> is known to dislike Haley, and the c=
ampaign clearly doesn=E2=80=99t view her as an asset in the final stretch a=
s they scramble to galvanize low-propensity, first-time voting bros, rather=
 than trying to win over suburban voters. <br/><br/>Certainly, it=E2=80=99s=
 hard to imagine that was the demographic Trump had in mind while cruising =

 in the results at a private watch party at Mar-a-Lago with his inner circl=
e, family, closest supporters, and donors. His fan base will be at the Palm=
 Beach convention center, where he=E2=80=99ll arrive when he=E2=80=99s read=
